content:
Formal **reproducible research** norms grew from **statistics** and **psychology** replication debates — the idea that published claims should survive independent reruns with the same protocol.

In **computational science**, early practitioners saved **random number generator states** and **compiler flags** because floating-point and parallel hardware introduced subtle drift. The **Netlib** and **LAPACK** era established that identical inputs plus identical software stacks should yield identical outputs on CPUs — a bar ML still chases on GPUs.

**Monte Carlo methods** (1940s onward) made **seeds** everyday vocabulary: simulations repeat only when the seed and algorithm match. **Pseudo-random** generators (linear congruential, Mersenne Twister) replaced true random hardware for efficiency and control.

The **ML reproducibility crisis** discussions of the **2010s** highlighted that many papers omitted seeds, data splits, and code. Venues and benchmarks began requiring **code submission**, **fixed splits**, and **environment files**.

**Containerization** (Docker, 2013+) and **dependency lock tools** (pip-tools, Poetry, conda-lock) made **environment pinning** practical for teams. **Experiment trackers** (MLflow, Weights & Biases, Neptune) standardized logging seeds and git commits alongside metrics — linking the statistical tradition to modern MLOps.

Today, **reproducibility & seeds** sit at the intersection of **experiment tracking**, **evaluation discipline**, and **deployment auditability** — not a single library feature, but a workflow habit.

timeline.label: Monte Carlo & PRNGs (1940s–1990s)
timeline.description: Simulation research formalizes seeded pseudo-random sequences for repeatable stochastic experiments.

timeline.label: Reproducible computational science (1990s–2000s)
timeline.description: Journals and HPC communities push documented software versions and deterministic CPU numerics.

timeline.label: ML reproducibility scrutiny (2010s)
timeline.description: Benchmark papers and replication studies expose missing seeds, splits, and code in ML publications.

timeline.label: Containers & experiment tracking (2010s–present)
timeline.description: Docker images, lock files, and MLflow/W&B make logging seeds and environments routine in industry workflows.
